posts.title: Most sellers think kitchen prep starts with a renovation.

head.caption:
Most sellers think kitchen prep starts with a renovation.

It doesn't. It starts with one corner.

The one where the mail piles up. Where the toaster lives. Where the stand mixer hides. Every kitchen has it.

Clear that one corner — completely — and you change how a buyer reads the whole home. The difference between "someone lives here" and "someone cares about this place."

It's also the cheapest thing on the prep list.

If you're thinking about selling in Highland Park, University Park, or Preston Hollow, send me the address. I'll walk through what I'd actually clear first — and what I wouldn't touch.

#DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #HighlandParkTX #UniversityParkTX #ParkCitiesRealEstate #PrestonHollow #DallasLuxuryHomes #MelissaMettryRealEstate #SellerPrep

posts.title: The most expensive seller mistake I see in Park Cities homes isn't choosing the

head.caption:
The most expensive seller mistake I see in Park Cities homes isn’t choosing the wrong agent.

It’s the renovation that didn’t need to happen.

A $40K kitchen 60 days before listing rarely returns $40K. Buyers see a clean, updated kitchen, not a story about how much was spent. Appraisals look at comps, not your remodel receipts.

Meanwhile, $4K of strategic prep, staging the spaces that matter most in photos, clearing the kitchen “stuff zone,” consistent lighting, paint touch-ups, and refreshed landscaping, often delivers the same final sales price.

The difference is whether the money shows up in the buyer’s emotional connection to the home or in your closing statement.

If you’re thinking about renovating before listing in Highland Park, University Park, or Preston Hollow, DM me the address before you start. I’ll tell you what’s worth doing and what isn’t.

#DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #HighlandParkTX #UniversityParkTX #ParkCitiesRealEstate #PrestonHollow #DallasLuxuryHomes #MelissaMettryRealEstate #SellerPrep

posts.title: Buying and selling at the same time can feel overwhelming — especially in Park C

head.caption:
Buying and selling at the same time can feel overwhelming — especially in Park Cities, where the next home is rarely on the market when you need it. 🏡

I put together a short guide for sellers who are also buyers. How I time the two transactions, what I check before either home lists, and how I keep you out of "bridge loan or scrambling for a rental" territory.

If a move is on your radar, message me your timeline. This is a good place to start.

#DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #HighlandParkTX #UniversityParkTX #ParkCitiesRealEstate #PrestonHollow #DallasLuxuryHomes #MelissaMettryRealEstate

posts.title: Most moves to Dallas start with one question: where? 🤍

head.caption:
Most moves to Dallas start with one question: where? 🤍

From the outside, Dallas looks like one city. In practice, it's twenty different neighborhoods, each with its own pace, school system, commute, and lifestyle. The right one for you isn't on a list — it depends on what your week actually looks like.

So before I send anyone listings, I want to know a few things. What time does your morning start, and where do you need to be by 9? Whose schools matter most — your kids', a parent's place, your office? What does Saturday afternoon look like when nothing's on the calendar?

The answers reshape the map. A young professional family moving from Chicago lands in a totally different part of Dallas than a couple downsizing from New York. Same city, completely different streets.

If you're thinking about a move — anywhere from this summer to next year — send me your timeline, what you do, and what your daily life looks like. I'll come back with the three or four streets I'd actually consider for you.

#MovingToDallas #RelocatingToDallas #DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #DallasNeighborhoods #DFW #DallasTexas #MelissaMettryRealEstate

posts.title: Before real estate, I spent years in private banking. 🤍

head.caption:
Before real estate, I spent years in private banking. 🤍

It's where I learned that money decisions are rarely about money.

They're about a daughter starting kindergarten. A parent moving closer. A career pivot that no one outside the family knows is coming. The actual question on the table is almost never "what does the spreadsheet say?" It's "what does the next ten years of our life look like, and how do we not get it wrong?"

That's the conversation I always wanted to be in. And real estate — done with care — is the same conversation. A home is where most of that life is going to happen. The pricing, the inspection, the contingencies, the close — that's the technical layer. Underneath is a family making a decision they will live with every morning.

The work I love most happens in homes, in showings, in pre-listing conversations, and in the quiet middle of a negotiation, when families are deciding out loud what they actually want. I built a practice in Highland Park, University Park, and Preston Hollow, where most of my clients are also making the biggest financial decision they'll ever make outside of a business sale.

If you're somewhere in that conversation — a move on the horizon, a sale you've been putting off, a question you don't yet know how to ask — send me a note. First, I want to understand what matters to you.

#DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #HighlandParkTX #UniversityParkTX #ParkCitiesRealEstate #PrestonHollow #DallasLuxuryHomes #MelissaMettryRealEstate

posts.title: Most of the work of selling a house happens before there's a price. 🤍

head.caption:
Most of the work of selling a house happens before there's a price. 🤍

When a family is thinking about listing, the first thing I want to know isn't what they paid for it or what their neighbor sold for. It's what's actually driving the move. A kid starting somewhere new. A parent who shouldn't be alone anymore. A career pivot they haven't said out loud yet. The reason behind the move shapes the strategy more than the spreadsheet does.

I want to know who's in the room when they make this decision — the spouse, the financial advisor, the adult kids who don't live in town anymore. The quiet vetoers are usually the ones who slow a deal in week six.

I want to know what the next house looks like, because a move-up runs at a different speed than a move-out-of-state. I want to know what "I made the right call" would feel like to them, ninety days from now. For some families that's a number. For others it's a closing date, a school start, or knowing the neighbor didn't get a discount.

And I want to know the worst version of how this could go. Inspections that surface something real. An appraisal that comes in low. The buyer who walks at the last minute. We name them out loud at the beginning. They have less power over a sale once they're in the room.

If a sale is somewhere on your horizon — a few months or a year out — let's have that conversation early. It costs nothing.

#DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #HighlandParkTX #UniversityParkTX #ParkCitiesRealEstate #PrestonHollow #DallasLuxuryHomes #MelissaMettryRealEstate

posts.title: Before I show a buyer a single house, I want to know five things. 🤍

head.caption:
Before I show a buyer a single house, I want to know five things. 🤍

The first is what "home" feels like once they walk in. Not the listing photo — the moment after the door closes. The light. The way sound carries. Whether anyone takes their shoes off without being asked. A house that doesn't feel right in the first thirty seconds rarely fixes itself by closing.

The second is where their life is actually centered. Kids' school, the office, an aging parent's place, the gym, the friends they see most weeks. The map between those points is the real shape of where they should live — and it almost never matches what they think they want on a first call.

The third is the worst version of how this could go. Over budget. Wrong school zone. The regret of buying right before they wanted to move again. Naming it early changes which houses I even show.

The fourth is their honest timeline — and what's driving it. A job that's already started, a lease ending in March, a school decision someone made in December. The real timeline is rarely the one printed in the email signature.

The fifth is who else is in the decision who isn't on the tour today. Spouses traveling, parents weighing in from another state, financial advisors. The people who aren't in the room are usually the people who change the deal.

If you're somewhere in that conversation — relocating to Dallas, a few months out, or further — send me a note. First, I want to understand what matters to you.

posts.title: What the inspection report doesn't say. 🤍

head.caption:
What the inspection report doesn't say. 🤍

A home inspection almost always comes back with multiple line items. The report doesn't tell you which ones actually change the deal. That's the conversation buyers really need.

In practice, every finding falls into one of three buckets:

🛑 Real ship-stoppers. Foundation movement past what the engineer will sign off on. A roof at or past its useful life. An electrical panel from a recall era. Evidence of long-term moisture inside walls. These end deals or trigger major price resets. Worth pausing the calendar over.

📋 Negotiating leverage. HVAC near the end of its lifespan. Water heater older than ten years. Polybutylene plumbing. Galvanized pipe sections. Aging windows. Surface drainage issues. None of these kill a sale on their own — but together they're worth real money in the right negotiation.

🪵 Age of the home. Hairline foundation cracks on an older build. Original wood windows that whistle in a north wind. Cast iron drain lines that have lasted seventy years. A pool finish that's due. Older homes have character — and character has wear. The inspection lists it; a good agent helps you weigh it.

What the report can't show you is which findings will actually matter the morning after closing. That's where having someone in the room who's read a ton of these matters more than any single page.

If you're in escrow or close to it — or just wondering what to expect — send me a note. We'll talk through what your specific inspection means.

#DallasRealEstate #DallasRealtor #DallasLuxuryHomes #HomeInspection #HomeBuyers #BuyersAgent #MovingToDallas #RelocatingToDallas #DallasNeighborhoods #MelissaMettryRealEstate

5 ways buyers lose the house. 🤍

Almost every deal that falls apart in Park Cities falls apart for the same handful of reasons. None of them are about the house. All of them are avoidable.

Here are the five I see most:

01 — Touring before pre-approval.
Sellers pick the buyer who already has the letter. Listing agents stop returning the call from the buyer who doesn't. The market here is too tight to lead with "I'll get pre-approved if I love it."

02 — Coming in too low.
Sellers don't counter. Other agents remember. A lowball offer doesn't just kill this deal — it makes you a less-credible buyer on the next one.

03 — Opening new credit mid-contract.
A new card or auto loan tanks your DTI overnight. The loan dies at the closing table — when there's no time left to fix it. Don't apply for anything new between contract and close.

04 — Treating inspection as a wishlist.
Asking for cosmetic fixes alongside the real ones hands the seller a reason to walk. Use inspection on the things that actually matter and let the rest go.

05 — Sleeping on the right one.
The best homes in Park Cities go in 48 hours. "Let me think about it overnight" is how you watch it close to someone else. If it's the one, decide like it's the one.

The work I do for buyers isn't only finding the home. It's keeping the deal alive long enough to close it.

If you're thinking about buying in Park Cities or the broader Dallas market — DM me "BUYER" before you start touring. We'll talk through your specific situation.

posts.title: The honest first question I ask new buyers isn't "what's your budget?" 🤍

head.caption:
The honest first question I ask new buyers isn't "what's your budget?" 🤍

It's: what kind of night do you actually want?

Because in Dallas, every neighborhood writes a different evening.

Some pockets are formal dinner — polished, generational, the table was set decades ago and everyone knows the builder. Some are backyard dinner, kids underfoot, charm that doesn't try. Some are cocktails-first — the restaurants are downstairs and your plans are already in motion before you change clothes.

Swipe for the map I walk every buyer through.

Six pockets, six different evenings — Park Cities, Preston Hollow, Bluffview / Greenway Parks, Lakewood / M Streets, Turtle Creek / Knox / Uptown, and Bishop Arts / Design District / Deep Ellum. Approximate price bands in the deck — they shift by lot, condition, and micro-location, so DM if you want the longer version.

The best Dallas neighborhood isn't the one with the prettiest house. It's the one that matches how you actually live.

DM me "NEIGHBORHOODS" and I'll send my per-area notes — schools, lot sizes, what's actually moving, and what to watch out for.

posts.title: What your Dallas home search is actually asking for. 🤍

head.caption:
What your Dallas home search is actually asking for. 🤍

Most buyers open the conversation with bedrooms, budget, and a couple of neighborhoods they've heard of.

The real question underneath all of it is lifestyle.

Here's the way I sort it for clients:

If you want privacy → look for separation, not just square footage. Preston Hollow, Bluffview, Greenway Parks. You're not buying space, you're buying distance from your neighbors.

If you want polish → look for reputation, proximity, and rhythm. Highland Park, University Park, Turtle Creek, Knox. You're buying a very specific Dallas pace.

If you want charm → look for trees, character, and lived-in beauty. Lakewood, M Streets, Greenland Hills, Kessler Park. You're buying homes that feel personal, not staged.

If you want energy → look for restaurants, movement, plans nearby. Bishop Arts, Lower Greenville, Deep Ellum, Design District, Uptown. You're shortening the distance between home and your weekend.

If you want flexibility → look beyond the listing photos. Lot quality, school paths, resale depth, micro-location. The best purchase is the one that still makes sense five years from now.

The right search doesn't start with bedrooms. It starts with the life you want — then it finds the address that supports it.

DM me "SEARCH" and I'll send my per-area notes — what's actually moving, what to watch out for, and what the listing photos won't tell you.

posts.title: Dallas home features that actually change daily life. 🤍

head.caption:
Dallas home features that actually change daily life. 🤍

Some upgrades photograph beautifully. Others quietly change how a home feels every single day.

These are the six I tell buyers to pay attention to — they don't always show up in the listing photos, but they're what you'll thank yourself for at 7 a.m. on a Tuesday:

01 — A real mudroom. Not a bench by the door. A real drop zone for bags, shoes, packages, sports gear, and daily chaos. Daily calm starts at the door.

02 — A kitchen that works for weeknights. Big islands are nice. Better question: can two people cook, unload groceries, and move around without bumping into each other?

03 — Natural light where you actually live. Pay attention to the rooms you use at 7 a.m. and 6 p.m. Morning and evening light change the whole feel of a house.

04 — Storage that matches real life. Holiday bins. Luggage. Golf clubs. Extra paper towels. If there's no place for it, it becomes visual clutter.

05 — A backyard with a reason to use it. Raw yard size isn't the whole story. Shade, privacy, usable patio space, and flow from the house matter more.

06 — A floor plan that gives people somewhere to go. Open concept is great until everyone needs a different kind of quiet. The best homes have both gathering spaces and escape spaces.

The right home shouldn't just impress on tour day. It should make your regular Tuesday better.

DM me "DAILY" and I'll tell you which features actually matter for the pocket of Dallas you're looking in.
